techniques.name: Wound Infection Management
techniques.subtitle: Recognize and treat infected wounds in the field

steps.instruction: Recognize infection: redness spreading from wound, warmth, swelling, pus, red streaks.
steps.helpDetail: Red streaks extending from the wound toward the body's core indicate lymphangitis — the infection is spreading to the bloodstream. This is a critical emergency. Fever with wound = systemic infection.

steps.instruction: Reopen and flush the wound with clean water — at least 1 liter under pressure.
steps.helpDetail: Use a water bottle with a pin-hole to create a pressure stream. Flush deep into the wound. Remove any debris or dead tissue with sterilized tweezers. This is painful but essential.

steps.instruction: Apply a warm, wet compress to draw out infection.
steps.helpDetail: Soak cloth in warm (not boiling) water. Apply to wound for 20 minutes, 4x daily. Warmth increases blood flow which brings white blood cells. Change dressing after each application.

steps.instruction: Apply natural antiseptics if no medical supplies available.
steps.helpDetail: Raw honey is a proven antibacterial — pack wound with it. Garlic paste has antibacterial properties. Pine resin (softened) has been used for millennia. Charcoal poultice draws toxins.

steps.instruction: Elevate the infected limb and keep the person hydrated.
steps.helpDetail: Elevation reduces swelling. Hydration supports immune response. Mark the boundary of redness with a pen/marker — if it expands, infection is advancing. Evacuation becomes critical.

techniques.name: Rib Fracture Management
techniques.subtitle: Stabilize and manage broken rib pain

steps.instruction: Confirm rib fracture: point tenderness on ribs, pain worse with breathing/coughing.
steps.helpDetail: Gently press each rib. Sharp pain at one point = likely fracture. If pain is on BOTH sides or across multiple ribs, suspect flail chest (medical emergency requiring evacuation).

steps.instruction: DO NOT wrap or bind the chest tightly.
steps.helpDetail: Old advice was to wrap ribs. This restricts breathing and causes pneumonia. Instead, support the injured side by holding a pillow or folded clothing against it when coughing.

steps.instruction: Practice deep breathing exercises every hour despite the pain.
steps.helpDetail: Shallow breathing from pain leads to pneumonia — the #1 complication of rib fractures. Take 10 deep breaths every hour. Hold a pillow against the injured side for support.

steps.instruction: Position for sleep: sleep on the INJURED side.
steps.helpDetail: This seems counterintuitive but sleeping on the injured side splints the fracture naturally and allows the uninjured lung to expand fully. Use padding for comfort.

steps.instruction: Monitor for pneumothorax: sudden worsening, one-sided chest expansion.
steps.helpDetail: If a broken rib punctures the lung: sudden severe shortness of breath, one side of chest doesn't move with breathing, crackling sensation under the skin. EMERGENCY — position sitting upright, evacuate.

techniques.name: Choking Response (Complete Guide)
techniques.subtitle: Clear blocked airway — adult and child techniques

steps.instruction: Assess: can they cough, speak, or breathe? If yes — encourage coughing.
steps.helpDetail: A person who can cough forcefully has a partial obstruction. Do NOT intervene with back blows yet — their own coughing is more effective. Stand ready. If coughing weakens or stops, proceed to step 2.

steps.instruction: Give 5 sharp back blows between the shoulder blades with heel of your hand.
steps.helpDetail: Support their chest with one hand, lean them forward. Strike firmly between the shoulder blades. Each blow should be a separate attempt to dislodge the object. Check mouth after each blow.

steps.instruction: If back blows fail: 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver).
steps.helpDetail: Stand behind them. Make a fist, place above navel below ribcage. Grasp fist with other hand. Pull sharply inward and upward. Each thrust should be forceful and deliberate.

steps.instruction: Alternate: 5 back blows, then 5 abdominal thrusts until cleared.
steps.helpDetail: If victim becomes unconscious: lower to ground, begin CPR. Each time you open the airway to give breaths, look in the mouth for the object. If visible, sweep it out with a finger.

steps.instruction: For infants: 5 back blows with baby face-down on your forearm, then 5 chest thrusts.
steps.helpDetail: Support the head, lay face-down on your forearm (head lower than body). Strike between shoulder blades with heel of hand. Flip over and give 5 chest thrusts with 2 fingers on the sternum. NEVER do abdominal thrusts on infants.

techniques.name: Eye Injury (Field Treatment)
techniques.subtitle: Protect vision when medical help is far away

steps.instruction: Foreign object: flush eye with clean water for 15+ minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Tilt head so affected eye is down. Pour water across from inner corner (nose side) to outer corner. Do NOT rub the eye — this can embed particles deeper. Blink repeatedly underwater if near a clean water source.

steps.instruction: Impaled object: DO NOT REMOVE IT. Stabilize in place.
steps.helpDetail: Build a protective cup around the object (bottom of a cup, rolled cloth) and tape it in place. Cover BOTH eyes — both eyes move together, and movement of the uninjured eye moves the injured one. Evacuate immediately.

steps.instruction: Chemical exposure: flush continuously for 20+ minutes.
steps.helpDetail: For acids or alkali: immediate, prolonged flushing is critical. Alkali burns are worse than acid — they penetrate deeper. Do not try to neutralize chemicals. Just flush with water.

steps.instruction: Snow blindness: patch both eyes with dark cloth for 24 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Snow blindness is UV sunburn on the cornea. Extremely painful but usually temporary (24-48 hours). Keep eyes closed and covered. Cold wet cloth can soothe. Prevent with improvised goggles (slit in bark or tape).
